gist: Re (on-chain reinsurer) has built a protocol that channels stablecoin capital into the $1T global reinsurance market, offering depositors 12%+ yields backed by uncorrelated insurance premiums from 51 US carriers — not crypto-native feedback loops. Electric Capital's Avichal Garg argues Re represents the template for all future fintech: regulated front-end, smart-contract operations, on-chain capital markets as the back-end, giving startups a structural cost-of-capital advantage incumbents cannot replicate.

themes.theme: Re Protocol: Business Model & Capital Mechanics

insights.claim: Re is an on-chain reinsurer that backs 35 US insurance carriers with $500M in business today, targeting $1B within 7 months, with fewer than 12 employees — a radically higher operating leverage than legacy reinsurers with tens of thousands of staff.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Sub-12-employee company at $500M business run rate signals extreme unit economics; if the capital formation thesis holds, revenue per employee dwarfs incumbents and justifies a premium valuation multiple.
insights.quote: We back 35 insurance carriers with a half a billion in business. Um and should uh should grow to about a billion over the course of the next uh seven-ish months.

insights.claim: Re's capital structure has three tranches: a first-loss layer of ~$77M of the team's own capital, a higher-yielding illiquid mezzanine (reUSD junior), and a senior liquid layer (reUSD) that is minimum 2x over-collateralized and very remote from insurance risk.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: First-loss capital from the founders is a credibility signal. The senior tranche's double over-collateralization makes it a potential institutional-grade instrument, not retail speculation.

insights.claim: Stablecoin depositors in Re's senior tranche earn risk-free + 250 bps; the mezzanine tranche earns risk-free + 800–850 bps, translating to approximately 12% total yield at current rates.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: At 12% vs. 4% T-bill yields, the spread is ~800 bps — wide enough that even risk-adjusted it competes with many alternative credit instruments; important comparator when sizing DeFi yield strategies.
insights.quote: the senior the senior tranche is fixed. It's 250 basis points above risk-free, and then for the mez tranche, I think it's between 800 and 850

insights.claim: Re's yield is generated through implicit leverage: $1 of on-chain collateral posted in a regulated trust account collateralizes $5–$7 of written premium, effectively allowing the capital pool to earn 5x the risk-free rate before fees and loss ratios.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: This 5–7x premium leverage is the core math behind why stablecoin holders can earn teens returns; the key risk is loss-ratio spike — investors should monitor Re's combined ratio disclosures.
insights.quote: that dollar is levered right five to seven times. And so in effect that pool of capital can earn 5x the risk-free rate.

insights.claim: Re focuses exclusively on low-volatility, law-of-large-numbers lines — auto, home, workers' comp, commercial property — deliberately avoiding binary-outcome catastrophe risk (hurricane, wildfire) to make the business highly predictable and modellable.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: The deliberate avoidance of cat risk is an underwriting discipline signal; it limits upside but dramatically narrows tail risk for depositors, making the protocol safer to stress-test.

insights.claim: Re's reUSD receipt token is already accepted as collateral on Morpho, Fluid, and Pendle, enabling looping (borrowing against the receipt to re-enter the pool), pushing effective yields to high teens–low 20s% on a fully looped basis.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: DeFi composability is a TVL flywheel for Re; looping demand increases the capital Re can deploy, but introduces liquidation risk for the looper — not the protocol itself.

insights.claim: Bermuda reinsurers are already approaching Re to tap its on-chain capital pool, suggesting the protocol is transitioning from a standalone carrier to a common-pool capital marketplace analogous to Lloyd's of London.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Incumbent reinsurers using Re rather than competing with it is the key inflection signal; watch for formal partnerships or white-label announcements as a buy signal for the RE token.
insights.quote: Bermuda reinsurers came in reinsurers are approaching us to be able to tap that pool

insights.claim: Re's primary growth metric is TVL, not revenue; Karan spends most of his time on TVL formation and recruiting underwriters with multi-billion-dollar book relationships from traditional reinsurance.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: For token/protocol investors, TVL growth is the leading indicator; declining TVL is a red flag before it shows in yields or partner count.

insights.claim: Re is targeting $1B+ in premium by January 1, 2027 — the annual insurance market renewal date — treating that as the pivotal commercial milestone to establish itself as a serious market player.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Jan 1, 2027 is a hard check-in date; underperformance vs. $1B target would signal market-share traction problems ahead of the next renewal cycle.
insights.quote: we're gearing up for Jan 1st, 2027, which is when the insurance market kind of turns over and renews, to crack a billion um in premium.

themes.theme: Stablecoin Capital Markets: The $5T Yield-Hunting Wave

insights.claim: Avichal Garg (Electric Capital) predicts stablecoin supply will grow from ~$200B today to $5 trillion, and argues 20–80% of those holders will seek yield above T-bill rates, creating a massive new demand pool for structured on-chain yield products.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: A $5T stablecoin market with 20% yield-seeking = $1T hunting for instruments like Re's; protocols that lock in supply-side relationships early (insurance cos, mortgage originators) have structural first-mover advantage.
insights.quote: you're going to have today you have a couple hundred billion, you're going to end up with 5 trillion dollars of USD stablecoins on chain

insights.claim: Garg frames on-chain stablecoin markets as a direct replacement for the entire traditional capital-markets function of fintech — collapsing Blackstone-style debt tranching, pension fund distribution, and securitization into a single smart contract.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: If correct, every fintech with a large capital-markets team (lending, insurance, HELOC, credit cards) faces margin compression from on-chain competitors with no equivalent overhead.
insights.quote: you've collapsed that whole thing now into a smart contract. And the stablecoin markets are just the capital markets now.

insights.claim: Garg predicts that within roughly a decade, the distinction between 'on-chain' and 'off-chain' capital will dissolve, just as 'going online' became meaningless once the internet was ubiquitous — and expects this convergence within 5 years.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: A 5-year horizon for on-chain/off-chain convergence sets a concrete timeline for when institutional incumbents must have resolved their crypto-compliance posture — or cede ground.
insights.quote: I think there will come a day, somewhere in the next decade, where we just stop using that term.

insights.claim: The real yield from real-world insurance premiums flowing back to stablecoin depositors is fundamentally non-self-referential — it does not depend on crypto-asset prices, unlike Terra/Luna-type yields that collapse when the underlying asset falls.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: This is the critical risk differentiation: the yield is uncorrelated to crypto price cycles, which means Re-type products could retain yield in a bear market where CeFi/DeFi lending yields collapse.
insights.quote: This is not like a Terra Luna situation where it's all you know, exogenous to crypto and it's all self-referential and when it unwinds it unwinds in a terrible way.

insights.claim: The Berkshire Hathaway / Geico / Gen Re model — using insurance float as essentially levered investment capital — is the explicit template Re is replicating on-chain; Apollo/Athene is also cited as a parallel.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: This framing suggests Re's terminal value should be benchmarked against Berkshire's insurance+asset-management moat, not just other DeFi protocols — dramatically higher valuation ceiling.
insights.quote: Berkshire has Geico and has Gen Re that generate all of this premium float, ends up being invested. Apollo has Athene.

insights.claim: Garg argues that the entire supply-side DeFi stack (Morpho, Aave, Pendle, Gauntlet, Steakhouse) is now fully built, and the missing piece that finally activates the flywheel is demand — specifically, real-world yield sources that justify stablecoin deployment.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: If demand (real yield) is the last piece, protocols that credibly bridge real-world cashflows to DeFi should capture disproportionate TVL growth in 2026–2027.
insights.quote: The last thing that we needed was the demand side, right? It's like, why do the stable coin holders want to do this? Why do they want to put it into the plumbing in the first place? And it's to get yield.

themes.theme: Fintech Disruption: On-Chain Operational Efficiency Moat

insights.claim: Garg's core investment thesis: in financial products, even a 10–30 basis point efficiency improvement causes the entire market to switch providers, because counterparties are rational and numbers are large — making DeFi's structural cost reduction a winner-take-all dynamic rather than a marginal upgrade.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: This logic applies across any high-volume financial product (mortgages, credit cards, HELOCs): the first on-chain provider to hit regulatory acceptance and competitive rates doesn't just gain share — it can price out incumbents entirely.
insights.quote: a 10 basis point improvement in something like insurance is dramatic, right? Like that is enough where you can go outcompete everybody else

insights.claim: Garg argues incumbents (banks, insurers, mortgage brokers) will adopt AI relatively quickly because AI vendors (OpenAI, Anthropic) are actively deploying forward engineering teams to help them — but stablecoin/on-chain capital markets adoption will lag years behind because no equivalent forward-deployment exists.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: On-chain fintech startups have a wider and longer moat from stablecoin capital markets than from AI alone; investors should weight the capital-markets advantage heavily, not just the AI workflow story.
insights.quote: there's no forward-deployed engineering organization that's going into insurance companies and teaching them how to do startups to do stablecoins right now

insights.claim: The three competitive levers in any lending/insurance fintech are: (1) base rate (Fed funds — uncontrollable), (2) underwriting quality (largely converged — dangerous to over-optimize), and (3) operational efficiency (the only real startup lever).
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating any fintech startup should ask: what is their operational efficiency advantage, not their claims of better underwriting — the latter is almost always a trap.
insights.quote: the place where you have leverage as a business to ultimately compete as a startup is to be more operationally efficient

insights.claim: Re operates with fewer than 12 employees heading toward multi-billion dollars in insurance market participation — cited alongside Uniswap (100 employees vs Nasdaq's 10,000) and Hyperliquid (14 people vs Coinbase's thousands) as evidence of the crypto-enabled small-firm phenomenon.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: The headcount-to-market-size ratio is an early-stage productivity benchmark; comparable ratios at Uniswap/Hyperliquid became signals of protocol moat before broad recognition.

insights.claim: Reinsurance's operational edge can be compounded: AI workflows that ingest risk data faster than incumbents at origination — cutting through the chain of intermediaries from insured to capital — create responsiveness advantages that translate to lower combined ratios.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Watch for Re (or competitors) announcing AI-driven underwriting throughput metrics as a leading indicator of competitive differentiation vs. legacy reinsurers.

themes.theme: Lloyd's of London as the DeFi Blueprint

insights.claim: Garg argues that Lloyd's of London — a 330-year-old institution with a governance council, diverse underwriter syndicates, a shared capital pool, and secondary market trading of risk — is structurally isomorphic to a DeFi protocol like Morpho or Aave.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: If this framing holds, Lloyd's market share (~$60B GWP) is the benchmark for Re's protocol-layer ceiling — and the governance structure gives a template for token design that has survived centuries.
insights.quote: I was like, 'Holy [__] this looks like a DeFi protocol... You squint at it and you're like, This looks kind of like Morpho and Aave'

insights.claim: Re explicitly models its governance token on Lloyd's governance structure: the token controls acceptable counterparties, lines of business, capital requirements, common pool size, release timing, and economics — giving large holders enormous leverage over trillion-dollar capital flows.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: RE token value is not speculative — it controls allocation of an eventually multi-billion capital pool. Token holders are de facto governors of a regulated capital marketplace, analogous to Lloyd's Names.
insights.quote: it's a governance token for the re ecosystem. It's intended, you know, as we discussed kind of emulate Lloyd's of London governance

insights.claim: Lloyd's of London holds licenses in practically every country and has country-specific legislation enabling local carriers to transact on the network — Re explicitly sets this as its North Star regulatory template.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: The regulatory moat Lloyd's has built over 330 years is exactly what Re needs to replicate; progress on international licensing should be watched as a milestone.
insights.quote: That's the North Star for us.

insights.claim: Garg draws a direct historical analogy between Amazon using internal infrastructure (AWS origin story) and Re potentially becoming the platform layer for all reinsurers wanting access to on-chain capital — the 'act two' of solving your own problem then selling the solution to competitors.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: If Re executes this AWS-for-insurance playbook, its revenue ceiling expands from $1B-level underwriting to a platform tax on the entire $1T reinsurance market — a 10–100x revenue expansion.
insights.quote: if you solve it for yourself and you really get the flywheel going, then you can go offer that as your AWS to everybody else

themes.theme: RE Token: Value Accrual & Governance Design

insights.claim: The RE governance token launched at the time of this episode; it controls the common capital pool's investment policy, counterparty eligibility, lines of business, and economics — giving large token holders power over capital allocation analogous to Lloyd's Names (capital contributors).
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Unlike most DeFi governance tokens that govern fee switches over low-revenue protocols, RE governance controls a capital pool with real insurance premiums — the economic stakes behind governance are unusually high.

insights.claim: Karan frames RE token value accrual as coming from controlling the world's largest insurance capital pool over time, not from a direct fee-switch — arguing that the economic power of directing tens of billions of capital is itself the value, not a token buyback.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Investors seeking direct cash-flow yield from the token may be disappointed; the value thesis is governance optionality over a growing capital pool — more equity-like than bond-like.
insights.quote: there is a a sum of economics and cash flows that can perform multitude of economic actions that that show value here

insights.claim: Re has 51 active insurance trees (counterparties) as of the recording and expects to reach hundreds, which progressively dampens earnings volatility through diversification — a law-of-large-numbers argument for yield stability.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Counterparty count is an underappreciated risk metric; fewer than ~30 carriers means idiosyncratic carrier failure could cause yield volatility; the 51 figure and growing trajectory are stabilizing signals.
insights.quote: we've got 51 active insurance trees today. We'll get to the hundreds in short order

insights.claim: Global reinsurance premium is approximately $1 trillion annually; total insurance premiums (health, life, property & casualty) are $7–8 trillion annually; together this constitutes roughly 12% of global GDP.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: A $1T addressable market in reinsurance alone, with Re at $500M run-rate, implies Re has captured ~0.05% of TAM — massive runway even before moving into primary insurance.
insights.quote: a trillion in reinsurance premium that's processed annually

insights.claim: Garg extends the insurance-as-GDP-percentage argument further: if government services (military, police, fire, Medicaid) are treated as implicit insurance, the true 'insurance' share of the economy is closer to 80% of GDP, not 12%.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: A thought-experiment framing, not a trade. But it reinforces the thesis that insurance/risk-transfer is a foundational economic primitive — and digitizing it on-chain is categorically larger than a fintech niche.
insights.quote: I actually like. It's not like 12% of GDP, it's actually like 80%

insights.claim: Traditional reinsurance capital comes exclusively from pension funds, sovereigns, ultra-high-net-worth individuals, and family offices — entirely inaccessible to retail or DeFi participants until Re.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: Re is democratizing an asset class that was structurally gated to institutional capital; this could attract significant inflows from yield-hungry retail stablecoin holders who have no other route to insurance-linked returns.
insights.quote: who's supplying capital to this market right now, it's pension funds, it's sovereigns, it's ultra-high net worth individuals, and certain family offices

insights.claim: Insurance risk carved up by Re is geographically uncorrelated (US auto + home + workers comp across states), sector-uncorrelated, and crypto-price-uncorrelated — making it a genuine diversifier in a DeFi portfolio that is otherwise highly correlated to ETH/BTC.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: For DeFi portfolio construction, Re exposure functions like a zero-beta yield sleeve — valuable precisely because it provides yield in bear markets when other DeFi yields compress.
insights.quote: completely uncorrelated with equities, with crypto, and the like

insights.claim: Re's float investment model — premium collected but not yet paid out acts as free-float invested at the risk-free rate — combined with the implicit 5–7x leverage creates a compounding return structure, not a one-time premium.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: The float model means Re's yield is partially funded by investment income (even at 4–5% risk-free), not solely underwriting margin — this makes yields more stable across insurance cycles.
insights.quote: insurance companies and asset managers go together like peanut butter and jelly

themes.theme: Bear Market Timing & Contrarian Infrastructure Observation

insights.claim: Garg observes that Real World Asset / DeFi infrastructure is coming together precisely in a bear market — when few are paying attention — and by the time participants notice, the bull market will have already begun, echoing the pattern of every prior DeFi cycle.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: Classic counter-cyclical entry signal: if Re and similar RWA-yield protocols are building meaningful TVL and carrier relationships in a bear market, the risk/reward for early positioning is favorable before retail attention returns.
insights.quote: it's the depth of the bear market, so nobody's paying attention. Uh and so by the time people figure this out, we'll be like kind of raging bull again, and people will be like, 'Oh, yeah, why wasn't I paying attention 18 months ago?'

insights.claim: Garg says all of the DeFi infrastructure supply-side components (Morpho, Aave, Pendle, Gauntlet, Steakhouse risk curation) are now production-ready and battle-tested — the rails exist and the last piece is real-yield demand, which is now arriving.
insights.speaker: Avichal Garg
insights.so_what: The implication is that TVL in real-yield DeFi protocols could inflect sharply as demand catches up to existing supply infrastructure — a non-linear rather than linear growth path.
insights.quote: The plumbing is in place to make the flywheels work.

insights.claim: The 'DeFi mullet' architecture (fintech in the front, DeFi capital markets in the back) — already live in Coinbase with Morpho (Bitcoin loans) and Athena (yield) — is cited as the template for Re to become a consumer savings product via similar distribution.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Coinbase integration would be a step-function TVL catalyst for Re; watching for distribution partnerships with major retail on-ramps is a key trade trigger.

insights.claim: Karan sees a natural long-term path from reinsurance to broader insurance and eventually to asset management — the same convergence already visible in Berkshire/Apollo — but deliberately avoids designing for it now, focusing on the reinsurance vertical first.
insights.speaker: Karan Saroya
insights.so_what: The optionality to expand into primary insurance and asset management is embedded in the protocol design even if not activated — this embedded optionality should be valued in terminal multiples.
insights.quote: If you think about what reinsurers and insurance companies become in the limit, they become the largest asset managers in the world
